prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Yarr, ye be askin' why the sky be blue, eh? Well, gather 'round and I'll tell ye the tale, me hearties!

When the sun, that mighty ball o' fire, shines its brilliant light upon our fine Earth, its rays be spreadin' out like the treasure o' a thousand galleons. This light, me buckos, be made up o' all the colors o' the rainbow, from red to violet.

Now, the air we breathe, it be full o' tiny particles, like wee specks o' dust and water droplets. When the sunlight hits these particles, it be scatterin' every which way, like cannonballs fired from a ship. But here's the trick, ye see - the shorter the wavelength o' the light, the more it gets scattered by these particles.

Blue light, with its short wavelength, be scattered more than the other colors. So, when we gaze up at the heavens, we be seein' the blue light that's been scattered all around us, paintin' the sky a brilliant blue canvas.

So there ye have it, me hearties! The sky be blue because o' the scatterin' o' light by the wee particles in the air, and the shorter wavelength o' blue light makes it stand out like a treasure on the open sea! Drink up, and may the winds o' knowledge always be at yer back!
